naturalMeaning: The fixed grill itself — brick-built in many backyards, or a portable metal one on an apartment balcony.

examples.text: A churrasqueira já tá quente.
examples.translation: The grill is already hot.

naturalMeaning: The long metal skewer meat is threaded onto and laid across the grill, as opposed to a small stick.

examples.text: Passa o espeto de picanha aqui.
examples.translation: Pass the picanha skewer over here.

naturalMeaning: Diminutive of espeto — usually a single-serving skewer, also the word for street-vendor meat skewers sold at bars and fairs.

examples.text: Quero mais um espetinho de coração.
examples.translation: I want one more chicken-heart skewer.

naturalMeaning: "Na brasa" (over the embers) is the phrase that shows up on menus everywhere — cooked directly over hot coals, not open flame.

examples.text: Essa carne foi feita na brasa.
examples.translation: This meat was cooked over the embers.

examples.text: Falta carvão, alguém pode comprar mais?
examples.translation: We're low on charcoal, can someone buy more?

naturalMeaning: The traditional seasoning for churrasco — big crystals patted onto the meat, not table salt.

examples.text: Passa o sal grosso na picanha antes de assar.
examples.translation: Season the picanha with coarse salt before grilling it.

examples.text: Coloca a carne na tábua para cortar.
examples.translation: Put the meat on the board to carve it.

naturalMeaning: The single most iconic Brazilian cut — grilled whole, curved into a C-shape with the fat cap facing the fire, then sliced across the grain at the table.

examples.text: A picanha é sempre a primeira a ir pro espeto.
examples.translation: The picanha is always the first thing to go on the skewer.

naturalMeaning: A thin, flavorful cut that grills fast — a churrasco favorite for people who don't want to wait for the bigger cuts.

examples.text: A fraldinha fica pronta rapidinho.
examples.translation: The fraldinha is ready really fast.

naturalMeaning: Leaner and more delicate than picanha, often grilled whole and sliced thin.

examples.text: Você já provou a maminha daqui?
examples.translation: Have you tried the maminha here?

naturalMeaning: Almost always the first thing to hit the grill — quick to cook, so guests have something to nibble on while the bigger cuts finish.

examples.text: Corta a linguiça pra gente já ir beliscando.
examples.translation: Cut up the sausage so we can start snacking.

naturalMeaning: Skewered and grilled whole, usually with just salt — a beloved side dish for many, and something first-timers are gently dared to try.

examples.text: Alguém quer coração de galinha?
examples.translation: Does anyone want chicken hearts?

naturalMeaning: Cooked low and slow for hours, often overnight for a real gaúcho-style churrasco — not a quick cut.

examples.text: A costela leva umas seis horas no fogo.
examples.translation: The ribs take about six hours over the fire.

examples.text: A alcatra é mais barata que a picanha.
examples.translation: Alcatra is cheaper than picanha.

naturalMeaning: Comes from zebu cattle, common in Brazil — fatty and gelatinous, needs long, slow cooking to become tender.

examples.text: O cupim desmancha na boca quando fica pronto.
examples.translation: Cupim falls apart in your mouth once it's done.

examples.text: Prefiro o contrafilé com bastante gordurinha.
examples.translation: I prefer the contrafilé with plenty of fat on it.

examples.text: As asas de frango sempre acabam primeiro.
examples.translation: The chicken wings always run out first.

examples.text: A paleta de cordeiro tá uma delícia.
examples.translation: The lamb shoulder is delicious.

naturalMeaning: Feminine is 'churrasqueira' (same spelling as the grill itself — context tells them apart). Being named churrasqueiro for the day is a small honor, not just a chore.

examples.text: Hoje eu sou o churrasqueiro.
examples.translation: Today I'm the one grilling.

naturalMeaning: Whoever's yard, apartment, or building rooftop it's happening at — not necessarily the one grilling.

examples.text: Fala com o dono do churrasco se pode trazer alguém.
examples.translation: Ask the host if you can bring someone along.

naturalMeaning: Manioc (cassava) flour toasted with butter, bacon, onion, sometimes egg or banana — the near-mandatory side that goes on top of everything.

examples.text: Não tem churrasco sem farofa.
examples.translation: There's no barbecue without farofa.

naturalMeaning: Diced tomato, onion, and pepper in vinegar and oil — spooned over grilled meat to cut the richness.

examples.text: Passa o vinagrete pra mim, por favor.
examples.translation: Pass me the vinagrete, please.

naturalMeaning: Grilled directly on the churrasqueira, wrapped in foil or straight on the grate — a churrasco classic, distinct from Italian-style garlic bread.

examples.text: Bota o pão de alho na churrasqueira também.
examples.translation: Put the garlic bread on the grill too.

naturalMeaning: A firm, salty cheese that holds its shape on the grill without melting away — sold on skewers by beach vendors too.

examples.text: Um espetinho de queijo coalho, por favor.
examples.translation: One grilled cheese skewer, please.

examples.text: Tem molho de pimenta caseiro na mesa.
examples.translation: There's homemade hot sauce on the table.

naturalMeaning: The question every churrasqueiro asks before slicing: how done do you want your piece.

examples.text: Qual é o seu ponto da carne?
examples.translation: What doneness do you want your meat?

examples.text: Pra mim, mal passado.
examples.translation: Rare for me.

naturalMeaning: The most commonly ordered doneness at a Brazilian churrasco.

examples.text: Eu quero a minha ao ponto.
examples.translation: I want mine medium.

examples.text: Meu pai só come bem passado.
examples.translation: My dad only eats it well done.

examples.text: Vamos assar a carne agora.
examples.translation: Let's grill the meat now.

examples.text: Já temperei a carne de manhã.
examples.translation: I already seasoned the meat this morning.

examples.text: Alguém já acendeu o fogo?
examples.translation: Has someone already lit the fire?

examples.text: Só o churrasqueiro pode virar a carne.
examples.translation: Only the grillmaster gets to flip the meat.

examples.text: Tira a linguiça do fogo, já tá pronta.
examples.translation: Take the sausage off the grill, it's already done.

naturalMeaning: The default expectation for a Brazilian churrasco — the host usually provides the meat, guests bring their own drinks or chip in.

examples.text: Posso levar bebida?
examples.translation: Can I bring drinks?

naturalMeaning: The informal social rule around churrasco contributions — nobody expects the host to cover everything alone.

examples.text: É assim: cada um leva o seu.
examples.translation: That's how it works: everyone brings their own.

examples.text: Vou no seu churrasco, o que eu levo?
examples.translation: I'm coming to your barbecue, what should I bring?

examples.text: Vou te convidar pro churrasco de domingo.
examples.translation: I'm going to invite you to Sunday's barbecue.

examples.text: Posso ajudar a arrumar a mesa?
examples.translation: Can I help set up the table?

examples.text: Deixa que eu ajudo a limpar depois.
examples.translation: Let me help clean up afterward.

naturalMeaning: Less an event than an institution — Saturday or Sunday afternoon, extended family, and no fixed end time.

examples.text: O churrasco de fim de semana é sagrado lá em casa.
examples.translation: The weekend barbecue is sacred at my house.

naturalMeaning: The augmentative of churrasco — a bigger, louder, more festive version, usually with lots of people.

examples.text: Vai ter um churrascão lá em casa no sábado.
examples.translation: There's going to be a big barbecue bash at my place on Saturday.

naturalMeaning: The restaurant version of churrasco — waiters circulate with skewer after skewer until you flip your table card to "no more, thanks."

examples.text: Vamos naquele rodízio de carnes hoje?
examples.translation: Shall we go to that all-you-can-eat barbecue place today?

naturalMeaning: Literally "ground fire" — meat skewers planted at an angle around an open pit fire instead of a raised grill, the traditional gaúcho method.

examples.text: No Sul, muita gente ainda faz churrasco no fogo de chão.
examples.translation: In the South, lots of people still do barbecue over an open ground fire.

naturalMeaning: A churrasco held on the flat concrete rooftop (laje) of a house — very common in urban and peripheral neighborhoods without a backyard.

examples.text: Sextou! Churrasco na laje hoje.
examples.translation: It's Friday! Rooftop barbecue today.

translation: person from Rio Grande do Sul; southern Brazilian barbecue style
naturalMeaning: As an adjective, "churrasco gaúcho" refers to the slow, salt-only, ground-fire style from Brazil's deep South, seen as the most traditional version.

examples.text: O churrasco gaúcho não usa quase nenhum tempero além do sal.
examples.translation: Gaúcho-style barbecue uses almost no seasoning besides salt.

naturalMeaning: Literally "styrofoam fridge" — where the beer everyone brought stays cold.

examples.text: Bota a cerveja na geladeira de isopor.
examples.translation: Put the beer in the cooler.

examples.text: Que delícia essa picanha!
examples.translation: This picanha is so good!

examples.text: Prova essa, tá no ponto!
examples.translation: Try this one, it's cooked just right!

translation: you did a great job with the meat
naturalMeaning: A casual compliment aimed straight at the churrasqueiro — "mandar bem" means to nail it / do a great job.

examples.text: Parabéns, você mandou bem na carne hoje!
examples.translation: Nice work, you nailed the meat today!
